name: Rallidae
description: Rallidae is a cosmopolitan family of small to medium-sized terrestrial or marsh-dwelling birds, including rails, crakes, coots, and gallinules. Many species are secretive and more often heard than seen.

children.name: Rallus
children.description: Water rails — secretive, laterally compressed marsh birds built to slip silently through dense reedbed.

children.name: Rallus aquaticus
children.description: The vattenrall — one of Sweden's most heard but rarely seen birds.

children.name: Rallus elegans
children.description: The largest North American rail, with a chestnut breast and heavily barred flanks.

children.name: Rallus obsoletus
children.description: A large rail of Pacific coastal saltmarshes from California to Mexico.

children.name: Rallus longirostris
children.description: A long-billed rail endemic to mangrove forests of South America.

children.name: Rallus crepitans
children.description: An abundant rail of Atlantic and Gulf Coast saltmarshes.

children.name: Rallus limicola
children.description: A small, richly coloured rail of freshwater marshes across North America.

children.name: Rallus semiplumbeus
children.description: A critically endangered rail endemic to high-altitude wetlands of the Colombian Andes.

children.name: Rallus antarcticus
children.description: A small, dark rail of southern Argentina and Chile.

children.name: Rallus wetmorei
children.description: A critically endangered rail confined to Venezuelan mangrove patches.

children.name: Rallus caerulescens
children.description: A handsome blue-grey rail of eastern and southern African marshes.

children.name: Rallus madagascariensis
children.description: A rail endemic to the marshes of eastern Madagascar.

children.name: Rallus tenuirostris
children.description: A medium-sized rail of highland marshes in central Mexico.

children.name: Rallus nelsoni
children.description: A rail of Pacific saltmarshes from Mexico to Peru.

children.name: Rallus levipes
children.description: A rail of California coastal saltmarshes, a focus of conservation management.

children.name: Rallus beldingi
children.description: A rail of Baja California saltmarshes.

children.name: Rallus ripleyi
children.description: A little-known rail of South American marshes.

children.name: Lewinia
children.description: Lewin's rails — slender Indo-Pacific rails closely related to Rallus.

children.name: Lewinia pectoralis
children.description: A richly patterned rail of wet grasslands and coastal heathlands in Australia.

children.name: Lewinia striata
children.description: A widespread rail of freshwater marshes from South Asia to northern Australia.

children.name: Lewinia mirifica
children.description: A little-known rail restricted to Luzon in the Philippines.

children.name: Gallirallus
children.description: Forest and island rails — a predominantly Indo-Pacific genus remarkable for repeated evolution of flightlessness.

children.name: Gallirallus australis
children.description: A large, flightless rail endemic to New Zealand with a bold, inquisitive temperament.

children.name: Gallirallus philippensis
children.description: One of the most widespread rails, distributed from the Philippines across the Pacific to Fiji.

children.name: Gallirallus torquatus
children.description: A boldly barred rail of the Philippines, Sulawesi, and the Moluccas.

children.name: Gallirallus sylvestris
children.description: A Critically Endangered flightless rail on Lord Howe Island.

children.name: Gallirallus okinawae
children.description: A flightless rail endemic to Okinawa, listed as Endangered.

children.name: Habroptila
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Invisible Rail.

children.name: Habroptila wallacii
children.description: A flightless rail of sago swamps on Halmahera, Indonesia.

children.name: Nesoclopeus
children.description: A genus of large, poorly known rails from Pacific islands.

children.name: Nesoclopeus woodfordi
children.description: A large, weak-flying rail endemic to the Solomon Islands.

children.name: Nesoclopeus poecilopterus
children.description: An extinct flightless rail from Fiji, last recorded in the late 19th century.

children.name: Hypotaenidia
children.description: A genus of island rails from the Pacific and Australasia, formerly included in Gallirallus.

children.name: Hypotaenidia owstoni
children.description: A flightless rail extirpated from Guam by the brown tree snake, surviving in captivity.

children.name: Hypotaenidia rovianae
children.description: A little-known rail endemic to the New Georgia Islands, Solomon Islands.

children.name: Hypotaenidia insignis
children.description: A rail of New Britain with distinctive pink legs.

children.name: Himantornis
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Nkulengu Rail.

children.name: Himantornis haematopus
children.description: A large, long-legged rail of West and Central African lowland rainforest.

children.name: Megacrex
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the New Guinea Flightless Rail.

children.name: Megacrex inepta
children.description: A large, flightless rail of lowland forests and swamps in New Guinea. It is rarely observed and poorly known.

children.name: Aramides
children.description: Wood-rails — large, long-legged Neotropical rails of forested wetlands.

children.name: Aramides cajaneus
children.description: The most widespread wood-rail, from Mexico to northern Argentina.

children.name: Aramides calopterus
children.description: A little-known wood-rail restricted to humid foothill forests of the western Amazon.

children.name: Aramides saracura
children.description: An Atlantic Forest endemic of south-eastern Brazil, Paraguay, and Argentina.

children.name: Aramides ypecaha
children.description: The largest wood-rail, found in marshes of the Pampas region.

children.name: Aramides axillaris
children.description: A wood-rail of mangroves from Mexico to north-western South America.

children.name: Aramides wolfi
children.description: A rare wood-rail of the Chocó region, classified as Vulnerable.

children.name: Crex
children.description: A genus containing the Corncrake and African Crake.

children.name: Crex crex
children.description: A migratory rallid of hay meadows, now rare across much of Europe.

children.name: Crex egregia
children.description: A small crake of sub-Saharan African grasslands and marshes.

children.name: Amaurornis
children.description: Bush-hens and waterhen, widely distributed across Asia and the Pacific.

children.name: Amaurornis phoenicurus
children.description: One of the most common rallids in Asia.

children.name: Amaurornis akool
children.description: A plain brown rallid of dense riverine vegetation.

children.name: Amaurornis olivaceus
children.description: A uniformly olive-brown rail of forest understory in the Philippines and New Guinea.

children.name: Amaurornis moluccanus
children.description: A bush-hen of mangroves from the Moluccas to northern Australia.

children.name: Gallicrex
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Watercock.

children.name: Gallicrex cinerea
children.description: A large rallid of Asian wetlands, with a striking black breeding male.

children.name: Poliolimnas
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the White-browed Crake.

children.name: Poliolimnas cinereus
children.description: A small crake with a bold white eyebrow, found from Southeast Asia to Australia.

children.name: Porzana
children.description: Spotted crakes — small to medium-sized rallids of dense freshwater marsh vegetation.

children.name: Porzana porzana
children.description: A secretive summer visitor to European marshes.

children.name: Porzana carolina
children.description: The most abundant North American rallid.

children.name: Porzana albicollis
children.description: A medium-sized crake of South American wetlands.

children.name: Porzana fusca
children.description: A small crake of marshes across South and East Asia.

children.name: Porzana paykullii
children.description: A striking crake of eastern China and Southeast Asia.

children.name: Porzana tabuensis
children.description: A uniformly dark crake found from the Philippines to the Pacific.

children.name: Porzana spiloptera
children.description: A small crake of temperate South American marshes, classified as Vulnerable.

children.name: Porzana flaviventer
children.description: A tiny crake found from the Caribbean to Argentina.

children.name: Porzana marginalis
children.description: A small, striped crake of African marshes.

children.name: Porzana bicolor
children.description: A crake of the eastern Himalayas and Southeast Asia.

children.name: Porzana exquisita
children.description: A poorly known crake of East Asian wetlands.

children.name: Porzana palmeri
children.description: An extinct flightless crake from Laysan Island, last recorded in 1944.

children.name: Porzana sandwichensis
children.description: An extinct flightless crake from the island of Hawai'i.

children.name: Hapalocrex
children.description: A monotypic genus of tiny crakes separated from Porzana.

children.name: Hapalocrex flaviventer
children.description: A tiny crake with a bright yellow breast, found from the Caribbean to Argentina.

children.name: Micropygia
children.description: A monotypic genus of small, brightly spotted crakes.

children.name: Micropygia schomburgkii
children.description: A tiny crake with bold white spots on the upperparts.

children.name: Zapornia
children.description: A genus of small to minute crakes resurrected from synonymy with Porzana.

children.name: Zapornia pusilla
children.description: One of the world's smallest rallids, barely larger than a sparrow.

children.name: Zapornia parva
children.description: A small crake breeding in reedbeds from Central Europe through Central Asia.

children.name: Zapornia tabuensis
children.description: A small, uniformly dark crake distributed from the Philippines to New Zealand.

children.name: Zapornia flavirostra
children.description: A common African crake of marshes and wetlands, with a yellow bill and red legs.

children.name: Zapornia flaviventris
children.description: A small crake of South American wetlands with a yellow breast.

children.name: Zapornia olivieri
children.description: A rail endemic to Madagascar, found in marshes and reedbeds. Classified as Endangered.

children.name: Zapornia bicolor
children.description: A small crake of the Himalayas and Southeast Asia, with a black tail and rufous underparts.

children.name: Laterallus
children.description: A genus of tiny to small Neotropical crakes.

children.name: Laterallus jamaicensis
children.description: One of North America's most secretive birds.

children.name: Laterallus albigularis
children.description: A small crake of wet lowland forests from Honduras to western Ecuador.

children.name: Laterallus melanophaius
children.description: A small crake of wet grasslands across lowland South America.

children.name: Laterallus exilis
children.description: A tiny crake of humid lowland forest floor from Central America through the Amazon.

children.name: Laterallus leucopyrrhus
children.description: A striking crake of south-eastern South America with a red head and white belly.

children.name: Laterallus spilonota
children.description: A small, dark crake endemic to the Galápagos Islands.

children.name: Laterallus ruber
children.description: A small, reddish crake of marshes from Mexico to Costa Rica.

children.name: Laterallus viridis
children.description: A small crake of South American grasslands with a russet crown.

children.name: Laterallus leucopterus
children.description: A little-known crake of Amazonian floodplains, with white wing patches.

children.name: Laterallus levraudii
children.description: A small crake of south-eastern South America with a rufous face.

children.name: Sarothrura
children.description: Flufftails — a genus of small, skulking African rails with fluffy tails.

children.name: Sarothrura pulchra
children.description: The most widespread African flufftail.

children.name: Sarothrura elegans
children.description: A forest-floor flufftail detected by its booming call.

children.name: Sarothrura rufa
children.description: A flufftail of dense wetland vegetation with a rich chestnut breast.

children.name: Sarothrura ayresi
children.description: A rare flufftail of high-altitude wetlands in Ethiopia and southern Africa, classified as Critically Endangered.

children.name: Sarothrura lugens
children.description: A flufftail of Central African marshes, with distinctive long toes.

children.name: Sarothrura boehmi
children.description: A small flufftail of Central and East African wetlands.

children.name: Sarothrura affinis
children.description: A small flufftail of eastern and southern African highland grasslands.

children.name: Coturnicops
children.description: A small genus of tiny, sparrow-sized crakes.

children.name: Coturnicops noveboracensis
children.description: One of the most secretive birds in North America.

children.name: Coturnicops notatus
children.description: A tiny crake of South American grasslands.

children.name: Coturnicops exquisitus
children.description: A tiny crake of East Asian wetlands.

children.name: Gallinula
children.description: Moorhens — medium-sized rallids of open water and emergent vegetation.

children.name: Gallinula chloropus
children.description: A familiar sight on every Swedish pond and urban waterway.

children.name: Gallinula galeata
children.description: The New World counterpart of the Common Moorhen.

children.name: Gallinula tenebrosa
children.description: A large, dark moorhen of Australian and Indonesian wetlands.

children.name: Gallinula melanops
children.description: A small gallinule with bold white spots on the flanks.

children.name: Gallinula angulata
children.description: Africa's smallest moorhen.

children.name: Gallinula sanctae
children.description: A moorhen of the Galápagos Islands.

children.name: Gallinula pacifica
children.description: A flightless gallinule from Savai'i, Samoa.

children.name: Gallinula silvestris
children.description: A flightless gallinule endemic to Makira in the Solomon Islands.

children.name: Gallinula nesiotis
children.description: A flightless moorhen from Tristan da Cunha.

children.name: Gallinula comeri
children.description: A flightless moorhen endemic to Gough Island.

children.name: Tribonyx
children.description: A genus of Australasian native-hens.

children.name: Tribonyx ventralis
children.description: A nomadic rallid of Australian wetlands.

children.name: Tribonyx mortierii
children.description: A large, flightless rail endemic to Tasmania.

children.name: Porphyrio
children.description: Swamphens and gallinules — large, often vibrantly coloured rallids.

children.name: Porphyrio porphyrio
children.description: A large, blue-purple swamphen of reedbeds across southern Europe and Africa.

children.name: Porphyrio madagascariensis
children.description: A swamphen of sub-Saharan African wetlands.

children.name: Porphyrio poliocephalus
children.description: A swamphen of South and Southeast Asia.

children.name: Porphyrio indicus
children.description: A swamphen of Southeast Asia and Indonesia.

children.name: Porphyrio pulverulentus
children.description: A swamphen endemic to the Philippines.

children.name: Porphyrio melanotus
children.description: A large swamphen found across Australasia.

children.name: Porphyrio martinicus
children.description: One of the most spectacularly coloured rallids.

children.name: Porphyrio alleni
children.description: A small, vividly coloured gallinule of African wetlands.

children.name: Porphyrio hochstetteri
children.description: A massive flightless gallinule rediscovered in 1948.

children.name: Fulica
children.description: Coots — robust, slate-black rallids with distinctive white bills and frontal shields.

children.name: Fulica atra
children.description: One of the most abundant waterbirds in Sweden.

children.name: Fulica americana
children.description: The most abundant coot in the Americas.

children.name: Fulica armillata
children.description: A South American coot with a red-orange band on the bill.

children.name: Fulica rufifrons
children.description: A coot with a reddish-orange frontal shield.

children.name: Fulica leucoptera
children.description: A coot with white-tipped secondaries.

children.name: Fulica alai
children.description: A coot endemic to the Hawaiian Islands.

children.name: Fulica ardesiaca
children.description: A coot of high-altitude Andean lakes.

children.name: Fulica gigantea
children.description: The world's largest coot.

children.name: Fulica cornuta
children.description: A remarkable high-Andean coot that builds stone nest mounds.

children.name: Fulica cristata
children.description: A coot with two red knobs on the frontal shield.

children.name: Fulica newtoni
children.description: An extinct coot from the Mascarene Islands.

children.name: Fulica caribaea
children.description: A coot of Caribbean islands, with a large white frontal shield extending to the crown.

children.name: Pardirallus
children.description: Medium-sized rails of Central and South America.

children.name: Pardirallus sanguinolentus
children.description: A slate-grey rail of marshes from Peru to Chile.

children.name: Pardirallus maculatus
children.description: A boldly spotted rail of marshes from Mexico to Argentina.

children.name: Pardirallus nigricans
children.description: A dark, sooty-brown rail of South American lowlands.

children.name: Pardirallus rythirhynchus
children.description: A blue-grey rail with a red-and-white bill.
